feedback.text: Hello, I just wanted to ask you to consider adding another note to your Digital Fairness Act. As many here are saying, it's regarding the permanent disabling of access to play the video games we have purchased. The number of times this has happened is countless, but I can name Battleborn and Lawbreakers as examples of games lost to time. Both the artistry of its developers, and my money (no refund given). I find that always-online access is very restrictive to games not only as an artform but as a product we invest money and time into these digital worlds. Thousands of hours and dollars PER game. They're often filled with so many ads and microtransactions, and while that is indeed an issue, I think the biggest problem is not even being able to keep what you spent once they shut the servers down. Self control can temper one's need of spending, but there's nothing I can do to avoid being swindled in THIS way. It's not about asking them to maintain servers til the heat death of the universe like the publishers seem to think/wishes to misdirect the EU, it's about sunsetting things with dignity, like letting us see family at an elder home, or keeping a playable memorabilia, even if it requires work to get going in the case of games which require many people for an optimal experience. For those who wish to keep the flame alive, they should be allowed some manner of ability to do so. For reasons of nostalgia, study, learning from mistakes or great gameplay, showing your children how things were like in your day and letting them try themselves... and above all, fun. There are many ways to solve this (via user-hosted servers with written non-liability for publishers), proven by past and present developers, especially if the problem is focused on from the get go, and publishers heavily dramatize the financial efforts required to pull this off because they've gotten far to comfortable doing as they wish. I believe in a free market, but their ways set such a bad precedent. It started small, but as I've grown up, it's only gotten worse every year. What they could do is figure out a way for us to keep our purchased items once the game's service had ended. This has been done by some already. Three positive examples I can quickly count are the paid offline re-releases of the mobile games (that side of gaming is often guilty of their gambling houses being shut down, but anyways;) - Animal Crossing: Pocket Camp Complete - MEGA MAN X DiVE Offline - METAL SLUG ATTACK RELOADED Publishers are so scared they'll lose less than infinite money, but they would honestly get more out of me. I've stopped given so many experiences a try because many inevitably die within a week to a year. It's ridiculous. I could go on but, I'll end it here. Thank you for reading.
